content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

May 22, 1995


Barbara Kiley
Committee to Elect Barbara Kiley
P.O. Box 1177
Yorba Linda, CA  92686

Re:  Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-159

Dear Ms. Kiley:

You have requested advice concerning the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTIONS

You are a candidate in a special election to fill a vacancy
for the 72nd Assembly District. You have asked if you can borrow
money from your father, then loan your campaign some or all of the
money. While speaking with you, you also asked if your father
could cosign a loan to you.

CONCLUSIONS

Under the provisions of the Act, you may not loan your
campaign some or all of the money received from your father in
excess of $1,000 during any special election cycle or special
runoff election cycle, nor may your father cosign a loan to you in
excess of this amount.


1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-95-159
Page 2


FACTS

     You are a candidate for the 72nd Assembly District.  This
seat is being vacated by Ross Johnson and the special election to
fill this vacancy will be set for later this summer.  You are
thinking about personally borrowing a large sum of money from your
father.  You have asked if you can loan your campaign some or
all of the money.  You have also asked if your father could cosign
a loan to you.

ANALYSIS

     Section 84216(a) provides that a loan received by a candidate
or committee is a contribution unless the loan is received from a
commercial lending institution in the ordinary course of business,
or it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not
made for political purposes.  Moreover, a person who makes an
enforceable promise to make a payment to or at the behest of a
candidate makes a contribution to the candidate.  (Section 82015.)
A person makes an enforceable promise to make a payment if he or
she cosigns a loan.  (Regulation 18216.)

     According to your facts, you intend to campaign in a special
election for the 72nd Assembly District.  Therefore, the
contribution limits of Section 85305 will apply to contributions
received by your campaign committee.  Section 85305(c) provides
generally that a candidate may not accept any contribution or loan
which exceeds $1,000 from a single source during any special
election cycle or special runoff election cycle.  Therefore, a
loan received from or cosigned by your father which will be used
for political purposes may not exceed $1,000 during the special
election cycle and may not exceed $1,000 during the special runoff
election cycle.

     If you have any further questions, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]

                                        By: Trish Mayer
                                            Political Reform Consultant
                                            Technical Assistance Division
